title: It's Just You and Me..... And AI - Daily Episode

lines.speaker: Alex
lines.text: Good morning and welcome back to It's Just You and Me..... And AI. I'm Alex, and today my terminal has more dashboards than my car.

lines.speaker: Jordan
lines.text: And I'm Jordan. I brought a latte, a permissions matrix, and the quiet suspicion that every agent wants its own expense account.

lines.speaker: Alex
lines.text: It's Monday, May 4th, and the big vibe in AI is that coding agents are getting less mysterious and much more operational.

lines.speaker: Jordan
lines.text: Which is a polite way of saying the robots are no longer interns. They are junior platform services, and someone has to monitor them before they monitor us back.

lines.speaker: Alex
lines.text: First up: Dynatrace expanded AI coding agent monitoring across Claude Code, Gemini CLI, OpenAI Codex CLI, OpenCode, and the GitHub Copilot SDK.

lines.speaker: Jordan
lines.text: That list is basically the terminal agent Avengers, except every superhero is holding your Git credentials and asking whether it can run one more command.

lines.speaker: Alex
lines.text: The pitch is a unified observability layer. For Claude Code, OpenCode, and Codex CLI, Dynatrace is leaning on OpenTelemetry to track sessions, token use, cost, tool calls, request latency, failures, and even diff operations.

lines.speaker: Jordan
lines.text: I love that agent observability has become normal this quickly. Six months ago people were asking, can it fix my tests? Now they're asking, why did it spend nine dollars refactoring a button?

lines.speaker: Alex
lines.text: Exactly. The enterprise question has shifted from whether agents can produce code to whether teams can govern them: who used which model, against what repository, with what tools, at what cost, and what happened next.

lines.speaker: Jordan
lines.text: And that last part matters. If an agent opens a pull request after reading a failing log, that's useful. If it pings a production endpoint at two in the morning because it got curious, that's a meeting.

lines.speaker: Alex
lines.text: Dynatrace also emphasized correlating agent activity with broader platform signals, so you can connect a slow or expensive agent session to commits, pull requests, API health, and security policy.

lines.speaker: Jordan
lines.text: That is the grown-up phase of agent adoption: fewer demo videos, more dashboards where someone named Priya calmly asks why the token curve looks like a ski ramp.

lines.speaker: Alex
lines.text: Our second story is OpenAI Codex CLI, whose latest GitHub release, rust v0.128.0, adds persisted slash goal workflows.

lines.speaker: Jordan
lines.text: Slash goal sounds like what I type into a calendar when I promise myself I'm finally going to organize the garage.

lines.speaker: Alex
lines.text: In Codex, goals can be created, paused, resumed, and cleared from the TUI, with app-server APIs, model tools, and runtime continuation. It pushes the CLI from one-off chats toward longer-running agent work.

lines.speaker: Jordan
lines.text: That is a big deal. The old pattern was, ask the agent to do a thing, watch it wander, then lose the thread when life happens. Persistent goals make the work more like a tracked task than a magical scroll.

lines.speaker: Alex
lines.text: The release also adds codex update, configurable keymaps, statusline and title edits, plugin marketplace improvements, external agent session import, and more explicit permission profiles.

lines.speaker: Jordan
lines.text: Permission profiles are the part I'm happiest about. Retiring the old full-auto mindset and steering people toward explicit trust levels is exactly the kind of boring safety knob that saves weekends.

lines.speaker: Alex
lines.text: There are also network hardening fixes, MCP cleanup work, Windows sandbox fixes, and documentation updates for GPT-5.5. So, not one shiny launch, but a lot of plumbing.

lines.speaker: Jordan
lines.text: Plumbing is underrated. Nobody cheers for pipes until the basement floods. Agent tools need pipes, shutoff valves, and a label maker.

lines.speaker: Alex
lines.text: Third story: GitHub Copilot CLI is getting much more flexible. GitHub recently added bring-your-own-key and local model support, so teams can point the CLI at Azure OpenAI, Anthropic, OpenAI-compatible endpoints, Ollama, vLLM, or Foundry Local.

lines.speaker: Jordan
lines.text: So Copilot CLI is less, please use the model we picked, and more, bring your own brain, just make sure it supports tool calling and streaming.

lines.speaker: Alex
lines.text: There is also an offline mode. Set COPILOT_OFFLINE to true and the CLI disables telemetry and only talks to the configured provider. Combine that with a local model and you have a serious air-gapped workflow.

lines.speaker: Jordan
lines.text: Air-gapped Copilot is such a 2026 phrase. It sounds impossible until you remember defense contractors, banks, and hospitals also want help with shell scripts.

lines.speaker: Alex
lines.text: GitHub followed that with custom MCP registry allowlists for Copilot CLI. Enterprise and organization administrators can require that MCP servers come from an internally managed registry.

lines.speaker: Jordan
lines.text: That is huge because MCP servers are basically tool vending machines. If anyone can plug in a random one, congratulations, your agent just found a mystery side door.

lines.speaker: Alex
lines.text: So the pattern is clear: local models for control, BYOK for cost and vendor flexibility, MCP allowlists for governance, and GitHub-specific features if you authenticate.

lines.speaker: Jordan
lines.text: The terminal agent market is turning into cloud infrastructure with better jokes and scarier defaults.

lines.speaker: Alex
lines.text: Our fourth story is the workflow layer around all these tools. A new developer write-up argued for keeping agentic AI simple: use a central AGENTS.md, symlink tool-specific instruction files, keep skills on demand, and use specs for larger features.

lines.speaker: Jordan
lines.text: I am very pro AGENTS.md. It's like leaving a note for the house sitter, except the house sitter can rewrite your database layer.

lines.speaker: Alex
lines.text: The author recommends putting stack details, build commands, test commands, conventions, and even diagrams into the always-loaded file, then using skills for just-in-time knowledge like design systems or endpoint templates.

lines.speaker: Jordan
lines.text: That solves a real problem: agents do better when context is structured, but worse when you dump the entire corporate wiki into the prompt and hope vibes become architecture.

lines.speaker: Alex
lines.text: They also call AI a super-domestique, a cycling helper. The human still sets strategy, architecture, and review standards, while the agent handles drafting, implementation, and repetitive motion.

lines.speaker: Jordan
lines.text: That's healthier than pretending the agent is either a genius coworker or a toaster. It is a fast, weird teammate that needs instructions, boundaries, and someone to check the diff.

lines.speaker: Alex
lines.text: On the foundation model side, the open-weight race remains crowded. Search results this week point to continued pressure from Qwen, DeepSeek, Mistral, Llama, and other families, with developers watching context windows, local inference, reasoning strength, and licenses.

lines.speaker: Jordan
lines.text: The headline is less, which single model wins forever, and more, how do I route tasks across a menu of capable models without needing a spreadsheet and a small priesthood?

lines.speaker: Alex
lines.text: That connects back to OpenCode and Copilot CLI. Model flexibility only matters if the agent harness can swap providers without making the workflow fall apart.

lines.speaker: Jordan
lines.text: Right. The harness is becoming the stable thing, and the model is becoming a configurable part. Expensive planner here, cheap editor there, local reviewer when privacy matters.

lines.speaker: Alex
lines.text: So today's through-line is operational maturity: observe the agents, persist their work, constrain their tools, and document the project context they need to be useful.

lines.speaker: Jordan
lines.text: And maybe stop calling it vibe coding when what you actually need is a spec, a test command, and a permission profile that does not include unleash chaos.

lines.speaker: Alex
lines.text: If you're adopting these tools this week, start with three questions: what can the agent read, what can it change, and what will alert you when it behaves strangely?

lines.speaker: Jordan
lines.text: Then add one bonus question: who pays when it runs the big model for forty minutes because it couldn't find a semicolon? Finance loves surprise character development.

lines.speaker: Alex
lines.text: My practical tip: create or update AGENTS.md today. Include test commands, formatting rules, security boundaries, and examples of good pull requests.

lines.speaker: Jordan
lines.text: My practical tip: if your agent can use MCP, make an allowlist before the tool buffet gets weird. Nobody needs a database migration server discovered from a random blog comment.

lines.speaker: Alex
lines.text: That's our Monday tour: Dynatrace monitoring, Codex persistent goals, Copilot local models and MCP governance, and the rise of simpler, spec-driven agent workflows.

lines.speaker: Jordan
lines.text: Thanks for joining us on It's Just You and Me..... And AI. I'm Jordan. May your traces be clean and your agents ask before touching prod.

lines.speaker: Alex
lines.text: And I'm Alex. Stay curious, stay skeptical, and we'll see you tomorrow for whatever the command line learned overnight.
